P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322-5662 
Robert M. Myers 
City Attorney 
1685 Main Street 
Administration 
322-5660 
Santa Monica, CA 
90401 
Dear Mr. Myers: 
Executive/Legal 
322-5901 
July 26, 1983 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice on 
Behalf of James Conn, 
Our Advice No. A-83-l64 
Your request for written advice on behalf of Councilmember 
James Conn presents the following facts for our consideration. 
FACTS 
1. 
James Conn is a member of the City Council of the City 
of Santa Monica. 
2. 
Councilmember James Conn is employed as a minister at 
The Church in Ocean Park (United Methodist). 
Appointment of a 
min~ster at a particular United Methodist Church is made by the 
United Methodist Church (Annual Conference). 
The Administrative 
Board of The Church in Ocean Park does not employ nor does it 
terminate a minister since those decisions are made at the 
discretion of the presiding Bishop. 
3. 
The Church in Ocean Park owns certain real property in 
the City of Santa Monica. 
The real property, which consists of 
a number of contiguous lots, is improved with three structures. 
One of the structures consists of a church facility. 
The 
remaining structures consist of single family structures. 
The 
two single family structures are not needed for day-to-day 
church operations and have been rented to non-profit community 
organizations. 
4. 
One of the organizations that rents church property is 
the Ocean Park Community Organizations (OPCO). 
OPCO has rented 
space at The Church since February I, 1980. 
Beginning with 
January 1983, the rent was $700 per month. 
The rent includes 


Robert M. Myers 
July 26, 1983 
Page 2 
the lease of one of the two single family structures and the 
right to use from time to time certain facilities of the main 
ch urch f acili ty. 
s. 
The governing board of The Church has developed over 
time a basic rental/use policy. 
The policy is as follows: 
a) 
The basic purpose and goals of the agency must be 
compatible with the principles of The Church in Ocean Park 
(United Methodist) and the United Methodist Church. 
b) 
The agency must be a non-profit SOl (c) (3) 
org aniza tion. 
c) 
The agency's rent must be negotiated and approved 
oy the Administrative Board of The Church or approved upon 
adoption of the annual budget of The Church by the Charge 
Conference of The Church. 
d) 
The agency must accept responsibility for oasic 
maintenance; larger maintenance efforts are negotiated. 
e) 
The agency must carry its own basic liability 
insurance. 
6. 
Rental rates and the salary of the minister are set at 
the annual meeting of the Administrative Board of the congre-
gation of The Church in Ocean Park. 
While procedurally the 
salary is set by motion at the Charge Conference, Rev. Conn's 
salary was actually set by the Annual Conference. 
The local church through the Administrative Board is 
responsible for meeting The Church's fiscal needs, including 
compensation of the minister. 
If The Church cannot meet its 
fiscal obligations, the Annual Conference can add salary 
support, but no such supplement has been received since July 
1976. 
The 1983 Annual Budget for The Church in Ocean Park is 
$70,668 with income of $70,680. 
7. 
OPCO is a non-profit corporation formed on approximately 
April 26, 1978. 
The current director of OPCO, Judy Abdo, and 
Councilmember Conn were among the original directors of the non-
profit corporation, but Rev. Conn has not been a director for 
over two years. 
(When he served as a director, Councilmember 
Conn received no compensation for his services.) 
The Articles 
of Incorporation provide for distribution of OPCO's assets to 
The Church in Ocean Park or some other SOl (c) (3) tax-exempt 
organization should it dissolve. 


Robert M. Myers 
July 26, 1983 
Page 3 
8. 
OPCO has been the recipient of grants from the City of 
Santa Monica to perform certain specified activities. 
These 
grants are administered through a contract entered into between 
the City of Santa Monica and OPCO. 
The source of grant funds 
are Community Development Block Grant Funds and General Revenue 
Sharing Funds. 
Last year these grants represented approximately 
95% of OPCO's budget. 
OPCO has indicated that even if it lost the funding which it 
receives from the City, it would continue to rent the premises, 
although it might sublease a portion of the premises if it were 
forced to cut back on its operations. 
However, since much of 
the City funds went for materials, a loss of the City contract 
would not result in a proportionate reduction in OPCO staffing. 
Ms. Abdo believes that replacement funding could be secured 
through fundraising. 
9. 
The fair rental value of the premises rented by OPCO 
from The Church is in excess of $700 per month. 
If OPCO vacates 
the premises, others have already indicated their interest in 
taking it over. 
QUESTIONS 
You have posed the following questions. 
1. 
May Councilmember Conn make, participate in making, or 
in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a 
governmental decision relating to OPCO in light of the fact that 
OPCO rents property from The Church in Ocean Park? 
2. 
May Councilmember Conn make, participate in making, or 
in any way attempt to use his ~fficial position to influence a 
governmental decision relating to OPCO in light of the fact that 
the Articles of Incorporation of OPCO provide that upon its 
dissolution its assets may be given to The Church in Ocean Park? 
3. 
If OPCO lawfully terminates its tenancy with The Church, 
at what point in time may Councilmember Conn make, participate 
in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to 
influence a governmental decision concerning OPCO? 
4. 
If OPCO amends its articles of incorporation to 
specifically designate some nonprofit organization other than 
The Church to receive its assets upon dissolution, at what point 
in time may Councilmember Conn make, participate in making or in 
any way attempt to use his official position to influence a 
governmental decision concerning OPCO? 


Robert M. Myers 
July 26, 1983 
Page 4 
ANALYSIS 
The Political Reform Actl/ dictates that a public official 
may not make, participate in making, or use his or her official 
position to influence any official decision in which he or she 
has a financial interest. 
Government Code Section 87100. 
A financial interest in a decision exists whenever the 
reasonably foreseeable effect of that decision will be both 
material and distinguishable (from the decision1s effect upon 
the public generally) on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the official has an 
investment of more than $1,000; 
(b) 
Any real property in which the official has an 
interest of more than $1,000; 
(c) 
Any source of income to the official of $250 or 
more during the preceding 12-month period; or 
(d) 
Any business entity in which the official is a 
partner, officer, employee, etc. 
Section 87103. 
In the case of Councilmember Conn, The Church in Ocean Park 
is not a business entity (Section 82005). 
Therefore neither 
Section 87103(a) or (d) applies. 
Rev. Conn does not have an 
ownership interest in the real property owned by The Church. 
Section 87103(b). 
However, The Church is a source of income to 
Rev. Conn of $250 or more during the last 12 months. 
OPCO is 
not a source of income to Rev. Conn and he has no other 
financial interest in it. 
Consequently, the only basis for requiring disqualification 
is if the decision on the OPCO contract will have a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect on The Church. 
The income 
from the space rented by OPCO constitutes approximately 12% of 
The Church's annual gross revenues. 
Clearly, loss of this 
income would constitute a material financial effect upon The 
Church. 
However, such loss does not appear to be foreseeable 
!/ Government Code Sections 81000-91014. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise 
specified. 


Robert M. Myers 
July 26, 1983 
Page 5 
because we are told that OPCO will continue to rent the space 
and sublease: if necessary. 
Even if OPCO did vacate the 
premises, we are advised that others are waiting to step in and 
rent the premises at the current rate. 
Based upon these facts~/ we must conclude that fore-
seeability is lacking and, therefore, disqualification is not 
required. 
This is true both as to the rental issue and the 
dissolution issue, since it is apparent that OPCO will not 
dissolve and will continue to rent. 
Given this conclusion, we 
need not address your third and fourth questions. 
Should you have any questions regarding this advice, please 
do not hesitate to call me at 916/322-5901. 
REL:km 
Sincerely, 
Robert E. Leidigh 
Counsel, Legal Division 
~ The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when 
it issues legal advice. 
Our advice is applicable only to the 
extent that the facts provided to us are correct and that all 
the material facts have been provided. 
Oglesby Opinion, 1 FPPC 
Opinions 71, 77 fn. 6, No. 75-083, July 2, 1975.

The meeting is set 
and chaired by the District Superintendent of the United

a 
figure 
not

salary.

Bishop in July 1973, Rev. Conn has always received the minimum 
salary permitted. 
The local church through the Administrative 
Board 
is

the church's fiscal needs, 
including 
compensation of the minister. 
If the church cannot 
meet 
its fiscal 
obligations, the Annual 
Conference can add 
salary support, but no such supplement has been received since 
July, 
1976. 
The 1983 Annual Budget 
for the Church in Ocean 
Park is $70,668.00 with income of $70,680.00. 
Councilmember 
Conn receives no 
other remuneration than 
his

In 
addition,

Church 
in 
Ocean Park in

for the church such as 
bookkeeping. 
8. 
OPCO 
has been the recipient of grants from the City 
of 
Santa 
Monica

between

opeo.

General Revenue Sharing Funds. 
9. 
The fair rental value of the premises rented by OPCO 
from the Church is in excess of $700 per month. 
FOR WRITTEN ADVICE 
Based upon the foregoing facts, Councilmember James Conn 
requests written advice on the following questions: 
1. 
May 
Councilmember Conn make, 
or 
any 
way 
to 
use 
participate in making, 
official position to 
fluence a governmental deci 
the 
fact 
that 
OPCO rents property from 
Park. 
to OPCO 
I 
of 
the Church 
Ocean 


Page 4 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
July 5, 1983 
2. 
May 
Councilmember Conn make, participate in making, 
or in any way attempt to use his official position to 
influence a governmental decision relating to OPCO in light of 
the fact that the Articles of Incorporation of OPCO provide 
that upon its dissolution its assets shall be given to the 
Church in Ocean Park. 
3. 
If the OPCO lawfully terminates its tenancy with the 
Church, at what point in time may Councilmember Conn make, 
participate in making or in any way attempt to use his 
official position to influence a governmental decision 
concerning OPCO. 
4. 
If 
OPCO 
amends 
its articles of incorporation to 
designate some other nonprofit organization other than the 
Church to receive its assets upon dissolution, at what point 
in time may Councilmember Conn make, participate in making or 
in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a

If you need any additional information in order to 
respond to this question, please do not hesi ta te to contact 
this office.

Given this conclusion, we need not address your third and fourth questions.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether Councilmember James Conn, who is employed as a minister at The Church in Ocean Park which receives rent from OPCO, must disqualify himself from participating in City decisions concerning OPCO's grants and contracts.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Councilmember Conn is not required to disqualify himself from OPCO decisions because while The Church is a source of income to him, loss of OPCO's rental payments is not reasonably foreseeable given that OPCO will continue renting and alternative tenants are available.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request (July 5, 1983) and FPPC response (July 26, 1983). Extraction based on the FPPC response letter. Footnote clarifies Commission does not act as fact-finder and advice is conditional on accuracy of provided facts.

QUESTION: You have posed the following questions. 
1. 
May Councilmember Conn make, participate in making, or 
in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a 
governmental decision relating to OPCO in light of the fact that 
OPCO rents property from The Church in Ocean Park? 
2. 
May Councilmember Conn make, participate in making, or 
in any way attempt to use his ~fficial position to influence a 
governmental decision relating to OPCO in light of the fact that 
the Articles of Incorporation of OPCO provide that upon its 
dissolution its assets may be given to The Church in Ocean Park? 
3. 
If OPCO lawfully terminates its tenancy with The Church, 
at what point in time may Councilmember Conn make, participate 
in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to 
influence a governmental decision concerning OPCO? 
4. 
If OPCO amends its articles of incorporation to 
specifically designate some nonprofit organization other than 
The Church to receive its assets upon dissolution, at what point 
in time may Councilmember Conn make, participate in making or in 
any way attempt to use his official position to influence a 
governmental decision concerning OPCO? 


Robert M. Myers 
July 26, 1983

CONCLUSION: Based upon these facts, we must conclude that foreseeability is lacking and, therefore, disqualification is not required. This is true both as to the rental issue and the dissolution issue, since it is apparent that OPCO will not dissolve and will continue to rent. Given this conclusion, we need not address your third and fourth questions.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice on whether Councilmember James Conn must disqualify himself from City decisions affecting OPCO, a nonprofit that rents property from The Church where Conn is employed as a minister. The FPPC concluded disqualification is not required because loss of rental income from OPCO is not reasonably foreseeable.
